_note: Synthetic mock data for educational and portfolio use only. No real patient information.

requests.raw_clinical_note: Patient is a 52-year-old male with 9 weeks of low back pain radiating down the left leg, consistent with L5 radiculopathy. Patient completed 8 weeks of physical therapy and has been taking ibuprofen 600mg three times daily, with no significant improvement in symptoms. Physical exam reveals a positive straight leg raise test on the left at 30 degrees, with decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome. No bowel or bladder symptoms. No prior lumbar imaging on file. Requesting lumbar spine MRI to evaluate for disc herniation.

Patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with 5 weeks of right-sided low back pain radiating to the posterior thigh and calf. Diagnosis of suspected L4-L5 disc herniation with radiculopathy. Physical exam shows positive straight leg raise on the right at 40 degrees. Patient reports pain is significantly limiting daily activities. No documentation of physical therapy or specific medication trial duration in the chart. No bowel or bladder dysfunction reported. No prior lumbar MRI on file. Requesting lumbar spine MRI.

structured_truth.diagnosis: Suspected L4-L5 disc herniation with radiculopathy

requests.raw_clinical_note: Patient is a 61-year-old male presenting with acute onset progressive bilateral leg weakness over the past 48 hours, accompanied by new-onset urinary retention. Patient reports saddle numbness. No history of trauma. Diagnosis of suspected cauda equina syndrome. No prior physical therapy attempted given acute presentation. No prior lumbar imaging on file. Requesting urgent lumbar spine MRI.

Patient is a 38-year-old female with chronic low back pain and known L5 radiculopathy, unchanged in character or severity over the past 6 months. Patient underwent a lumbar spine MRI 4 months ago for the same complaint, which showed a mild disc bulge without significant nerve compression. Physical exam shows mild decreased sensation in the L5 distribution, unchanged from prior visits. No red flag findings. Patient has continued physical therapy and oral analgesics throughout this period. Requesting repeat lumbar spine MRI due to ongoing pain, though no significant change in symptoms is documented since the prior MRI.

exam_findings: decreased sensation in L5 distribution, unchanged from prior
